quote: Hi. If you record your name and reason for calling, I'll see if this person is available.
stewart_read: Brian has a screening system on his line — he is not a passive pickup. The fact that Brandon cleared it and Brian stayed on the line, then answered with 'Hey. What's up, buddy?' (00:27), is a materially stronger positive signal than a cold pickup. This customer remembers the prior contact and chose to take the call. That warmth is an asset Brandon never explicitly acknowledged or leveraged.
coaching_implication: Coach Brandon to recognize and name the warmth when it's this clear — a brief 'I appreciate you picking up, I know you've got a screener on there' would have deepened the rapport before moving to scheduling. The signal was there; he walked past it.

quote: Just reaching back out about that design for your home. We didn't get a chance to meet with you last week, so I was calling to see if you'd be available. I have a specialist who can meet with you and help you explore your options.
stewart_read: Brandon skips the entire verify section and goes straight to scheduling. The codex's short-call compression provision (call_shape note) allows compression when 'existing records cover qualification' — but that provision requires the rep to have confirmed those records are current and complete. On a recycled lead where the prior appointment was missed or rescheduled, the qualification state is stale by definition. The reactive read holds: this is not strategic compression, it is a skip. The strategic hypothesis — that Brandon had confirmed qualification from prior records and was deliberately compressing — is unsupported because nothing in the call indicates he checked or referenced any prior data.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Brandon had complete qualification data from the prior appointment attempt and was legitimately compressing per the short-call provision. Rejected — no evidence he referenced or confirmed prior records, and a missed/rescheduled appointment is precisely the scenario where re-qualification is warranted.
coaching_implication: On recycled leads with a prior missed appointment, the manager should drill Brandon on the re-qualification question: 'What do you know about this customer's bill, roof, and title situation, and when did you last confirm it?' If the answer is 'from the prior record,' the next question is 'did you verify it's still current?' The short-call compression provision is not a blanket skip pass.

quote: And, you said Thursday works best?
stewart_read: Brandon misheard Brian's constraint. Brian had said 'anything except Thursday' (01:02 context) and Brandon reflected it back as 'Thursday works best' — the opposite of what was said. Brian had to correct him at 01:18 ('Anything except Thursday'). This is an active-listening failure on a simple scheduling exchange. On a warm, cooperative customer it didn't derail the call, but it introduced a small trust friction and required the customer to do corrective work.
coaching_implication: This is a low-stakes but clean coaching moment for active listening. The manager can replay 01:02–01:20 and ask Brandon: 'What did Brian say, and what did you say back?' The gap is obvious on playback. Tie it to the broader pattern: if Brandon is mishearing simple scheduling constraints, what else is he missing when the customer is giving him qualification signals?

quote: Okay. Good deal. Okay. Great. Alright. Thank you, Brian. We'll go over our solar programs and show you how much you could be saving with solar energy.
stewart_read: Brandon confirms the appointment and delivers a generic value statement, but the button-up protocol is almost entirely absent. Missing: one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'is that fair' verbal handshake, and text follow-up promise. On a re-booked appointment where the prior sit was already missed or rescheduled, the button-up protocol is the primary tool for protecting show rate. Skipping it here is the highest-risk gap in the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Brandon kept the close brief to avoid re-introducing friction on a warm customer who had already agreed. Rejected — the button-up elements are not friction-generating; they are show-rate protection. There is no strategic benefit to omitting them.
coaching_implication: Walk Brandon through the button-up checklist against this call. He hit zero of the five required elements. The manager should ask: 'If Brian doesn't show Wednesday, what did we do to protect against that?' The answer from this call is nothing. Drill the full button-up sequence until it is automatic on every booking, especially re-bookings.

quote: Okay. Good deal. Okay. Great. Alright. Thank you, Brian. We'll go over our solar programs and show you how much you could be saving with solar energy.
stewart_read: Credit where it is due: Brandon did not scope-creep. He did not explain panel technology, quote savings ranges, debate equipment, or try to sell solar on this call. His close is generic but it stays in setter territory — 'show you how much you could be saving' is a curiosity frame, not a feature pitch. Given that SESSION10 is flagged in the codex as a scope-creep negative example (Jake/Larry), it is worth noting that this Brandon call does not repeat that failure mode.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this explicitly in the 1-on-1. Brandon held scope. The coaching conversation should be: 'You stayed in your lane — good. Now let's talk about what you needed to do before you got to the close, because the lane was right but the car wasn't ready.'

quote: Yeah. Hopefully, what we're offering will fit your needs and can help you save some money on your energy bills.
stewart_read: No bill anchor was attempted at any point in this call. The word 'hopefully' in the close is the only reference to financial benefit, and it is framed as uncertain rather than grounded in Brian's actual bill pain. The codex requires bill capture AND emotional flip within the call structure. Here, neither occurred — the beat is entirely absent, not mishandled.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Brandon had bill data from the prior appointment attempt and chose not to re-surface it to keep the re-booking call short. Possible, but unsupported — nothing in the call references prior bill data, and 'hopefully' signals Brandon does not have a number to anchor to.
coaching_implication: Even on a 90-second re-booking call, a single bill reference ('you mentioned your bill was around $X last time — that's exactly what the specialist is going to work from') would have personalized the close and created a reason for Brian to show. The manager should ask Brandon: 'What do you know about Brian's bill?' If the answer is nothing, that is the gap. If the answer is 'it's in the record,' the next question is 'why didn't you use it?'

quote: Thanks for your time, Brian. Good deal, buddy. Thanks for reaching out. I appreciate it. You're welcome. See you this. Have a good day. Wednesday.
stewart_read: No text follow-up was promised on the call. The codex requires the rep to inform the customer that a text with appointment details will follow, and that text must include a tieback to something specific the customer said. Brandon did not promise the text, and there is no evidence one was sent. On a re-booked appointment, the text is a second confirmation touchpoint that materially reduces no-show risk.
coaching_implication: Add the text promise to the button-up drill. It is one sentence: 'I'll shoot you a text right after this with the details.' If Brandon is not sending personalized follow-up texts on re-bookings, the manager should check whether any text was sent after this call and use the result as the coaching anchor.
